text: You have the map awareness of a screensaver.
You fly like the autopilot filed a restraining order against you.
Somewhere a tutorial is still waiting for you to finish it.
You're living proof that confidence and skill are two entirely separate stats.
You treat the overview like it's optional reading.
Your ship insurance company knows you by first name.
You warp into fights the way toddlers walk into glass doors.
You could get lost in a one-system loop.
Your fitting choices read like a dare.
You aim like the crosshair owes you money and is actively avoiding you.
The kill feed uses you as a punchline.
You're the reason we double-check the fleet composition twice.
Your reaction time is measured in business days.
You tank damage like a paper towel tanks rain.
Hostile fleets consider you free content.
You call it a strategy; the killboard calls it a donation.
Your escape plan is just dying somewhere more convenient.
You navigate like the star map personally offended you.
Watching you pilot is like watching someone parallel park a whale.
You bring a knife to a gunfight, then drop the knife.
Your minimap is a decoration and everyone knows it.
You've been outsmarted by AI that was programmed to lose.
Your best play this month was logging off.
You alt-tab during fights and somehow play worse when you come back.
The respawn screen started a loyalty program because of you.
You're the only pilot who could lose a one-versus-zero.
Your battle plan has one step and it's panic.
You check the map like it's written in a language you refuse to learn.
You couldn't hit water if you fell out of a boat.
Your positioning suggests you're playing a different game entirely.
You peek corners like you're paying rent on the enemy's crosshair.
You're not the weakest link; you're the whole broken chain.
Your ego writes checks your gameplay keeps bouncing.
You have main character energy and NPC decision-making.
You lose duels to people who are also cooking dinner.
You'd struggle to win a turret section with aim assist on.
Your callouts are just narrated confusion.
You describe enemy positions like a weather forecast: vague and wrong.
You're fluent in exactly one tactic and it's the wrong one.
Every lobby's loot improves when you join, because you're the loot.
You farm deaths like they're a renewable resource.
You've made the walk back from the respawn point a cardio routine.
Your gaming chair deserves a better pilot.
You hit every branch falling out of the skill tree.
Your muscle memory has amnesia.
You clutch defeat from the jaws of victory on a schedule.
Your aim has a wander setting and it's stuck on.
You strafe like you're dodging responsibility, not bullets.
The enemy team lists you under assets, not threats.
You've been carried so often you legally qualify as luggage.
Your game sense went out for snacks mid-fight and never came back.
You're a spectator with a login.
Your highlight reel is just other people's kill cams.
You solo queue because no one can carry that much.
Your decision tree is a stump.
You've turned dying first into a personal brand.
Your threat level is somewhere below decorative.
You tactically retreat in every direction except the exit.
You read the battlefield like it's upside down and in Latin.
Your instincts have a loading screen.
You're the plot armor stress test that failed.
Scouts report your location as wherever the explosion was.
You'd lose a staring contest with a loading screen.
Your fleet role is officially listed as cautionary tale.
You press every button except the right one, in alphabetical order.
Your warp-ins have the precision of a dropped lasagna.
You treat friendly fire as a personality trait.
The tutorial NPC asked to be reassigned after meeting you.
You balance the matchmaking just by showing up.
Your kill count and your excuses are in a race, and the excuses are winning.
You've died to the same gate camp so often they named a shift after you.
Your overview settings are a mystery, even to you.
You orbit at the exact range where you're useless in both directions.
Your capacitor management is a countdown to embarrassment.
You jumped into the wrong system so hard it became your new home.
You mistake the warp button for a plan.
Your damage output is a rounding error.
You tackle like you're afraid of commitment.
Your drones gave up and started freelancing.
You fit ships like you lost a bet with the market.
Your safe spots are on everyone else's bookmarks.
You cloak up and still get spotted, which is honestly impressive.
Your directional scan has one direction: wrong.
You anchor on the FC like a lost duckling with lag.
Your bookmarks folder is just screenshots of places you died.
You broadcast for reps before the fight even starts.
Your mining laser has more kills than you do.
You undock like it's a surprise every single time.
Your jump clone has better judgement than you, and it's unconscious.
You take gates like they're personal challenges you keep failing.
Your killboard is a museum of bad ideas.
You align to nothing and warp to regret.
Your ping is an excuse; your gameplay is a confession.
You could fail the tutorial of a walking simulator.
Your buffoonery has structural integrity; it never collapses.
You're not unlucky; you're just consistent.
Aim assist took one look at your gameplay and resigned.
You've achieved the impossible: negative situational awareness.
Your legacy is a smoking wreck and a chat log full of oops.
